message.content:
Adversaries may stage resources referenced by malicious links to target users, often through spearphishing. These links can lead to credential harvesting or malware execution. Typically, adversaries set up HTML pages with client-side scripts, sometimes cloning legitimate sites like login pages to deceive users. They may also use domains similar to legitimate ones (homoglyphs, typosquatting) or trusted Platform-as-a-Service providers to host these malicious links, leveraging the credibility of these domains to avoid detection. URL shortening services are also commonly employed to obscure the malicious nature of the links. The impact includes unauthorized access, data theft, and potential system compromise.

Mitigation strategies include user education on recognizing phishing attempts, implementing email filtering to block suspicious links, using multi-factor authentication to protect against credential theft, and employing web filtering to block access to known malicious domains. Regularly updating and patching systems can also reduce the risk of malware execution. Monitoring and analyzing network traffic for unusual patterns can help detect and respond to these threats promptly.
